content:
Angular Angular Library Uikit React Component Library Web Development 6 min read 7 hours ago -- 170+ components. No zone.js. Full dark mode. Runtime theming. Angular 17.3 through 22. Angular has had a remarkable evolution over the last two years. Signals arrived in Angular 16, became stable in 17, and by Angular 19, zoneless change detection was production-ready. Yet most Angular UI libraries are still built the way they were in 2019 — NgModule-based, zone.js-dependent, with theme systems bolted on as an afterthought. NGXSMK UI Kit was built to change that. Press enter or click to view image in full size The Problem With Existing Angular UI Libraries Before diving into what NGXSMK does, it’s worth understanding the four recurring pain points that motivated its creation. 1. They still use NgModules. Every time a button is needed, ButtonModule gets imported. Then DialogModule. Then FormFieldModule. Before long, AppModule is 80 lines of imports and the bundle contains components that are never used. 2. They depend on zone.js. When building a performance-critical app and trying to go zoneless, most UI libraries actively fight back. They rely on ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck() in ways that assume zone.js will flush changes. 3. Theming is rigid. Many libraries compile a fixed set of SCSS variables at build time. Switching themes at runtime — say, based on a user preference stored in a database — means either shipping all themes in the bundle or hacking around it. 4. Animations are all-or-nothing. Either there are no animations, or a heavy animation library gets installed that lands in the bundle whether it’s used or not. NGXSMK was designed to solve every one of these problems. What Is NGXSMK UI Kit? NGXSMK is an open-source Angular component library built from the ground up for modern Angular (v17.3+). It ships as three focused packages: @ngxsmk/theme : Design-token engine — generates --ngxsmk-* CSS custom properties at runtime @ngxsmk/cdk : Low-level behaviors — click-outside, focus trap, scroll lock, live announcer @ngxsmk/core : 170+ standalone, signals-native components Here’s what makes each pillar special. Pillar 1: Signals-Native & Zoneless Every single component in NGXSMK uses Angular’s signals API: import { NgxsmkButton } from '@ngxsmk/core/button';@Component({ selector: 'app-root', imports: [NgxsmkButton], template: ``, }) export class App {} Under the hood, all component inputs use input(), outputs use output(), and two-way bindings use model(). No @Input() decorators. No EventEmitter. No subscription management. This means: • No zone.js required. All change detection is driven by signal reads. Angular knows exactly which components to update without monkey-patching browser APIs. • Perfect OnPush everywhere. Every component uses ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush. When signals change, only the affected subtree re-renders. • Works in both worlds. Whether an app uses zone-based or zoneless change detection, NGXSMK components just work. Setting up a zoneless environment takes two lines: export const appConfig: ApplicationConfig = { providers: [ provideZonelessChangeDetection(), withViewTransitions(), ], }; Pillar 2: Runtime Token Theming Most Angular libraries do theming at build time — they compile SCSS variables into the CSS bundle. NGXSMK does it at runtime using CSS custom properties. The theme engine (@ngxsmk/theme) takes a ThemeConfig and emits a flat set of --ngxsmk-* custom properties directly onto the document root: import { NgxsmkThemeService } from '@ngxsmk/theme';constructor(private theme: NgxsmkThemeService) {} ngOnInit() { this.theme.applyTheme({ preset: 'midnight', mode: 'dark' }); } Every component reads these properties directly: /* Inside a button component */ background: var(--ngxsmk-button-bg, var(--ngxsmk-color-primary-500)); color: var(--ngxsmk-button-color, var(--ngxsmk-color-on-primary)); border-radius: var(--ngxsmk-button-radius, var(--ngxsmk-radius-md));What this gives you: Runtime preset switching — switch from “Ocean” to “Midnight” with a single method call. No flash. No rebuild. User-level customization — store a user’s preferred theme in a database. Apply it on login. One line of code. Per-component overrides — every component exposes its own override hooks. A specific button can have a different background simply by setting --ngxsmk-button-bg on a parent element. Consistent control sizing — all single-line inputs (text input, select, combobox, multi-select, power-search) share one --ngxsmk-control-height token (default 2.5rem / 40px). Change one variable, resize the entire form suite. Light/dark/system modes — three strategies: class (toggle a class on the root), media (follow prefers-color-scheme), or system (auto). Switchable at runtime. Pillar 3: Tree-Shakable by Design NGXSMK ships every component as its own secondary entry point: // ✅ Deep import — only the button gets bundled import { NgxsmkButton } from '@ngxsmk/core/button';// ⚠️ Barrel import — works, but the bundler has more work to do import { NgxsmkButton } from '@ngxsmk/core'; Every package also has "sideEffects": false in its package.json. This tells the bundler it's safe to eliminate any re-export that isn't directly imported. The result: apps pay only for what they use. Pillar 4: Animations Without the Weight NGXSMK integrates with Motion — but as a completely optional peer dependency. Without motion installed, components work fine — they just don't animate. With it installed, a clean declarative API becomes available via two directives: NgxsmkAnimate — plays an enter animation once when the host element is rendered:
Hello
NgxsmkPresence — mounts/unmounts a template with coordinated enter/exit animations:
This animates in and out
Motion states are plain TypeScript objects: protected readonly enterMotion: NgxsmkMotionState = { initial: { opacity: 0, y: 8 }, animate: { opacity: 1, y: 0 }, transition: { duration: 0.2, easing: 'ease-out' }, };The built-in dialog, tooltip, and toast components already ship with animations wired up this way. prefers-reduced-motion is honored automatically — Motion jumps straight to the final state for users who prefer reduced motion. Most importantly: animations are lazy-loaded. The motion module is only pulled in when an animation actually runs. The initial bundle stays lean whether or not motion is installed. illar 5: Accessible by Default Accessibility isn’t a checkbox in NGXSMK — it’s baked into the component architecture. Custom form controls (checkboxes, radio buttons, switches) wrap native elements with a visually-hidden technique. This means: • ngModel and reactive forms work exactly as expected — no custom value accessors to implement. • Screen readers see real form elements. • Browser autofill, password managers, and accessibility tools all behave correctly. The CDK layer adds: • Focus trap — keeps keyboard focus inside modals and dialogs. • Live announcer — posts dynamic announcements to screen readers via ARIA live regions. • Scroll lock — prevents body scroll while overlays are open. • Reactive media queries — signal-based breakpoint tracking. The Component Catalog @ngxsmk/core ships 170+ components, including: • Layout: dividers, cards, panels • Feedback: alerts, progress bars, spinners, skeletons, toasts • Display: badges, tags, chips, avatars • Actions: buttons (multiple variants), icon buttons • Forms: form-field, text inputs, number input, select, combobox, multi-select, power-search, checkboxes, radios, switches • Navigation: tabs, accordions • Overlays: tooltips, dialogs/modals • Specialized: datepicker (via ngxsmk-datepicker + Luxon), telephone input (via ngxsmk-tel-input + libphonenumber-js) The datepicker and telephone input are isolated entry points — only bundled when explicitly imported. Angular Version Compatibility NGXSMK’s peer dependency is >=17.3.0, and the library is tested against every supported Angular major in CI: # .github/workflows/compatibility.yml # Builds @ngxsmk/theme, @ngxsmk/cdk, @ngxsmk/core against: # Angular 17.3, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22The key to forward compatibility: build with the lowest supported Angular version. A library compiled with ng-packagr for Angular 17.3 produces partial-Ivy output that Angular 22 can consume. The reverse is not true. Getting Started in 3 Steps Step 1: Install npm install @ngxsmk/core @ngxsmk/themeStep 2: Load the base stylesheet In the global stylesheet or angular.json: @import '@ngxsmk/theme/styles/ngxsmk.css';Step 3: Use a component import { NgxsmkButton } from '@ngxsmk/core/button';@Component({ selector: 'app-root', imports: [NgxsmkButton], template: ``, }) export class App {} No module registration. No zone.js configuration. No build-time theme compilation. What’s Next The library is stable at v1.1.0. On the roadmap: • More preset themes and a visual theme editor • Expanded overlay animations (sheet, dropdown, hover card) using NgxsmkPresence • Additional form field validation visuals and input types Found this useful? Drop a ⭐ on GitHub — it helps more developers discover the library. Tags: Angular · TypeScript · UI Library · Web Development · JavaScript · Open Source · Frontend · Angular Signals
